title: QCEW: Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages
text:
Comparing data access methods
By Isabella Jiang

Reading an full year of QCEW data using pubdata::get() takes a few seconds, which adds up doing multiple analysis over multiple years.
To improve efficiency, we can combine pubdata::path() (returns the exact disk path for a given table table) and arrow::open_dataset (analyze large, multifile dataset directly from disk without reading them fully into memory)



Code
# Reading an entire year and filter (2020)
time_entire <- system.time({
  df_full <- pubdata::get("qcew", "naics_ann_2020") %>%
    filter(agglvl_code == "10", own_code == "5") %>%
    select(area_fips, annual_avg_emplvl, avg_annual_pay)
  })

# arrow + filter
time_arrow <- system.time({
  df_arrow <- pubdata::path("qcew", "naics_ann_2020") %>%
    arrow::open_dataset() %>%
    filter(agglvl_code == "10", own_code == "5") %>%
    select(area_fips, annual_avg_emplvl) %>%
    collect()
  })

tibble(
  Method = c("Load entire table into memory", "Query subset using Arrow Dataset"),
  Runtime = c(time_entire[["elapsed"]], time_arrow[["elapsed"]])
)






Method
Runtime




Load entire table into memory
3.913


Query subset using Arrow Dataset
0.087

title: QCEW: Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages

Smallest states by employment in 2024

  filter(agglvl_code == "50") %>%
  left_join(pubdata::get("qcew", "meta_naics_area"), by = "area_fips") %>%
  select(state = area_title, employment = annual_avg_emplvl) %>%
  mutate(state = str_remove(state, " -- Statewide")) %>%
  arrange(employment) %>%

title: QCEW: Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages
text:
Employment growth rate by ownership (2020-2024)
By Isabella Jiang
Using pubdata::path() with arrow::open_dataset() for faster multiyear querying.


Code
df_arrow <- pubdata::path("qcew", "naics_ann_2020") %>% 
  dirname() %>%
  arrow::open_dataset() %>%
  filter(
    agglvl_code == "11",
    year %in% 2020:2024,
    stringr::str_detect(own_code, "^[1-3|5]$")) %>%
  left_join(pubdata::get("qcew", "meta_naics_own"), by = "own_code") %>%
  select(year, own_title, oty_annual_avg_emplvl_pct_chg) %>%
  collect()

ggplot(df_arrow, aes(x = year, y = oty_annual_avg_emplvl_pct_chg, color = own_title)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() + 
  labs(
    title = "Employment Growth Rate by Ownership (2020–2024)",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Annual Average Employment \n Percentage Change (%)",
    color = "Ownership Type"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

title: QCEW: Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages
section: The annual average pay trends of top 3 supersectors in 2024
text:
The annual average pay trends of top 3 supersectors in 2024
By Isabella Jiang


Code
top3_industries <- pubdata::get("qcew", "naics_ann_2024") %>%
  filter(agglvl_code == "13") %>%
  arrange(desc(avg_annual_pay)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 3) %>%
  pull(industry_code)

df_top3 <- map_dfr(2020:2024, function(y) {
  pubdata::get("qcew", paste0("naics_ann_", y)) %>%
    filter(agglvl_code == "13", industry_code %in% top3_industries) %>%
    group_by(year = y, industry_code) %>% 
    summarize(avg_annual_pay = mean(avg_annual_pay, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop_last") %>% 
    left_join(pubdata::get("qcew", "meta_naics_ind"), by = "industry_code") %>%
    select(year, industry_code, industry_title, avg_annual_pay)
})

ggplot(df_top3, aes(x = year, y = avg_annual_pay, color = industry_title)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar) +
  labs(
    title = "Average Annual Pay Trends for 2024's Top 3 Supersectors (2020–2024)",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Average Annual Pay",
    color = "Supersector"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

title: QCEW: Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages
section: Linear association between annual average annual pay and establishments
text:
Linear association between annual average annual pay and establishments
By Isabella Jiang


Code
df_pay_estabs <- map_dfr(2020:2024, function(y) {
  pubdata::get("qcew", paste0("naics_ann_", y)) %>%
    filter(agglvl_code == "10") %>%
    mutate(year = y) %>%
    select(year, avg_annual_pay, annual_avg_estabs)
})

lm_pay_estabs <- lm(avg_annual_pay ~ annual_avg_estabs, data = df_pay_estabs)

lm_pay_estabs %>%
  summary()



Call:

title: North American Industry Classification System (NAICS)
text:
On this page
   
  
  Overview
  Tables
  Columns
  Examples
  


Overview

The North American Industry Classification System (NAICS) is the standard used by Federal statistical agencies in classifying business establishments for the purpose of collecting, analyzing, and publishing statistical data related to the U.S. business economy.

Data source links.

Home



Tables
List of all tables in the collection.








Columns
Listing of all columns in each table.








Examples

title: County Health Rankings & Roadmaps (CHRR)
text:
Overview
  Tables
  Columns
  Examples
  
  Variables across years
  Rural premature mortality
  National adult smoking over time

title: County Health Rankings & Roadmaps (CHRR)
text:
Variables across years
Variables, descriptions and variable parts.


Code
all_year_vars <- pubdata::ls("chrr") %>%
  grepv("^raw_", ., invert = TRUE) %>%
  map(function(key) {
    pubdata::path("chrr", key) %>%
      open_dataset() %>%
      filter(fipscode == "00000") %>%
      select(year, variable, description) %>%
      collect() %>%
      separate_wider_regex(
        variable,
        cols_remove = FALSE,
        pattern = c(
          code = "^v\\d{3}",
          "_",
          race = "(?:race_[^_]+)?",
          "_?",
          value = ".*"
        )
      ) %>%
      relocate(year, variable, description)
  }) %>%
  bind_rows()

all_year_vars %>%
  datatable(filter = "top")

title: County Health Rankings & Roadmaps (CHRR)
text:
Rural premature mortality
County relationship between percentage of rural population and premature age-adjusted mortality.


Code
x <- pubdata::get("chrr", "2025")

x1 <- x %>%
  filter(countycode != "000") %>%
  filter(variable %in% c("v058_rawvalue", "v127_rawvalue")) %>%
  pivot_wider(id_cols = fipscode, names_from = variable) %>%
  rename(rural = v058_rawvalue, paam = v127_rawvalue)

x1 %>%
  ggplot(aes(rural, paam)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth()

title: Input-Output Tables - Bureau of Economic Analysis
text:
BEA-NAICS crosswalk
2023 I-O revision, 2017 NAICS revision.


Code
pubdata::get("bea_io", "2023_naics") %>%
  summarize(naics = str_c(naics, collapse = ", "), .by = c(sector, summary, u_summary, detail, title)) %>%
  datatable(filter = "top")

title: Input-Output Tables - Bureau of Economic Analysis
text:
GDP by commodity
GDP as the sum of final uses: consumption, investment, government spending, net export.
\[
Y = C + I + G + X - M
\]
Billions of dollars in 2022.


Code
x <- pubdata::get("bea_io", "2023_mu_use-bef-pro_sec_2022")
commodity_codes <- x %>%
  filter(core_matrix) %>%
  distinct(row_code) %>%
  pull()
x %>%
  mutate(
    col_name = case_match(
      col_name,
      "Personal consumption expenditures" ~ "C",
      "Private fixed investment" ~ "I1",
      "Change in private inventories" ~ "I2",
      "Exports of goods and services" ~ "X",
      "Imports of goods and services" ~ "M",
      "Government consumption expenditures and gross investment" ~ "G",
      "Total Final Uses (GDP)" ~ "Y"
    ),
    value = replace_na(value, 0)
  ) %>%
  filter(row_code %in% commodity_codes, !is.na(col_name)) %>%
  pivot_wider(id_cols = c(row_code, row_name), names_from = col_name) %>%
  mutate(I = I1 + I2) %>%
  select(row_code, row_name, Y, C, I, G, X, M) %>%
  { add_row(., row_code = "", row_name = "TOTAL", summarize(., across(where(is.numeric), sum)), .before = 1) } %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) round(x / 1000)))

title: Input-Output Tables - Bureau of Economic Analysis
section: Components of value added by industry
text:
Components of value added by industry
Use table subtotals satisfy the following accounting identity:
\[
\text{Total industry output} =
\text{Total Intermediate} + \text{Compensation of employees} + \text{Gross operating surplus} + \text{Net taxes}
\]
Table below shows percentage shares of total output by industry in 2022 at the sector level.


Code
x <- pubdata::get("bea_io", "2023_mu_use-bef-pro_sec_2022")
industry_codes <- x %>%
  filter(core_matrix) %>%
  distinct(col_code) %>%
  pull()
x %>%
  filter(
    row_name %in% c(
      "Total Intermediate", 
      "Compensation of employees", 
      "Gross operating surplus", 
      "Taxes on production and imports, less subsidies", 
      "Total Industry Output"),
    col_code %in% industry_codes
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    pct = round(100 * value / sum(if_else(row_name == "Total Industry Output", value, 0)), 1),
    .by = col_code) %>%
  filter(row_name != "Total Industry Output") %>%
  pivot_wider(id_cols = c(col_code, col_name), names_from = row_name, values_from = pct) %>%
  rename(intermediates = "Total Intermediate", 
         compensation = "Compensation of employees", 
         taxes = "Taxes on production and imports, less subsidies",
         surplus = "Gross operating surplus")

title: Business Dynamics Database (BDS)
text:
Overview
  Tables
  Columns
  Examples
  
  Metro vs non-metro exit rates
  Establishment exit and job creation
  Jobs in metro vs rural areas
  Metro vs rural growth
  Multi-establishment firms

title: Business Dynamics Database (BDS)
text:
Metro vs non-metro exit rates
How do the exit rates of establishments differ pre and post covid in metro vs non metros?


Code
# load the processed metro/nonmetro dataset
df <- pubdata::get("bds", "2022_met") %>%
  mutate(
    year = as.integer(year),
    estabs_exit_rate = as.numeric(estabs_exit_rate),
    metro_status = case_when(
      metro == "M" ~ "metro",
      metro == "N" ~ "nonmetro",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    covid_period = ifelse(year < 2020, "pre_covid", "post_covid")
  ) %>%
  filter(year >= 2016 & year <= 2022, !is.na(metro_status))

# summarise
summary_df <- df %>%
  group_by(year, metro_status) %>%
  summarise(avg_exit_rate = mean(estabs_exit_rate, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

# plot
ggplot(summary_df, aes(x = factor(year), y = avg_exit_rate, fill = metro_status)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = which(sort(unique(summary_df$year)) == 2020),
    linetype = "dashed", color = "red", size = 1
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Exit Rate of Establishments (Metro vs Nonmetro, 2016–2022)",
    y = "Average Exit Rate",
    x = "Year"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









The data indicate that metro establishments were more vulnerable to pandemic-related shocks, hence we see a larger establishment exit rate. Nonmetro establishments showed a smaller relative increase.This suggests that rural economies may have been somewhat more protected from pandemic disruptions which is an important factor to consider when working with business dynamics in rural areas.

title: Business Dynamics Database (BDS)
text:
Establishment exit and job creation
How does the job creation rate differ with establishment exit rate?


Code
# Load and preprocess correctly
df <- pubdata::get("bds", "2022_met") %>%
  mutate(
    job_creation_rate = as.numeric(job_creation_rate),
    establishment_exit_rate = as.numeric(estabs_exit_rate),
    metro_status = case_when(
      metro == "M" ~ "metro",
      metro == "N" ~ "nonmetro",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
  ) %>%
  filter(
    metro_status %in% c("metro", "nonmetro"),  # keep only metro and nonmetro
    !is.na(job_creation_rate),
    !is.na(establishment_exit_rate)
  )

# Summarize the relationship (linear regression by metro status)
relationship_summary <- df %>%
  group_by(metro_status) %>%
  do(tidy(lm(establishment_exit_rate ~ job_creation_rate, data = .))) %>%
  filter(term == "job_creation_rate") %>%
  select(metro_status, estimate, std.error, p.value)

print(relationship_summary)


# A tibble: 2 × 4
# Groups:   metro_status [2]
  metro_status estimate std.error      p.value
  <chr>           <dbl>     <dbl>        <dbl>
1 metro           0.236    0.0541 0.0000812   
2 nonmetro        0.395    0.0565 0.0000000133


Code
# Scatterplot with regression lines
ggplot(df, aes(x = job_creation_rate, y = establishment_exit_rate, color = metro_status)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "Job Creation Rate vs Establishment Exit Rate",
    subtitle = "Separate regression lines for Metro (Urban) and Nonmetro (Rural) Areas",
    x = "Job Creation Rate",
    y = "Establishment Exit Rate",
    caption = "Printed table shows slope estimates by area type"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()


`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'









The linear model above shows that in there is positive relationship between job creation rate and the establishment exit rate in both metro areas and non metro areas but the slope of non metro areas is higher than metro areas which signifies that there is higher instability in rural areas compared to urban areas.

title: Business Dynamics Database (BDS)
section: Jobs in metro vs rural areas
text:
Jobs in metro vs rural areas
What is the share of total employment in rural vs metro areas?


Code
df_met <- pubdata::get("bds", "2022_met") %>%
  mutate(
    emp = as.numeric(emp),  # total employment
    metro_status = case_when(
      metro == "M" ~ "metro",
      metro == "N" ~ "nonmetro",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(metro_status %in% c("metro","nonmetro"))

# Summed employment by type
emp_summary <- df_met %>%
  group_by(metro_status) %>%
  summarise(total_emp = sum(emp, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

# Add share
total_us_emp <- sum(emp_summary$total_emp, na.rm = TRUE)

emp_summary %>%
  mutate(share = total_emp / total_us_emp)






metro_status
total_emp
share




metro
4498865989
0.9607757


nonmetro
183668995
0.0392243






We can see that most of the jobs are concentrated in the metro areas with the employment share being 96% and the non metro’s share of employment being 3%

title: Business Dynamics Database (BDS)
text:
Metro vs rural growth
Which area is growing more(firms,employment) in metro vs non metro areas? This also accounts for the change before and after the COVID pandemic.


Code
# Load data (replace year key if needed)
df <- pubdata::get("bds", "2022_met") %>%
  mutate(
    metro_status = case_when(
      metro == "M" ~ "metro",
      metro == "N" ~ "nonmetro",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(metro_status))

# Summarize total firms and employment by metro status and year
summary_df <- df %>%
  group_by(year, metro_status) %>%
  summarise(
    total_firms = sum(firms, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_emp   = sum(emp, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Plot trends
p1 <- ggplot(summary_df, aes(x = year, y = total_firms, color = metro_status)) +
  geom_line(size = 1.2) +
  labs(title = "Firm Growth: Metro vs Nonmetro", y = "Number of Firms", x = "Year") +
  theme_minimal()

p2 <- ggplot(summary_df, aes(x = year, y = total_emp, color = metro_status)) +
  geom_line(size = 1.2) +
  labs(title = "Employment Growth: Metro vs Nonmetro", y = "Total Employment", x = "Year") +
  theme_minimal() + expand_limits(y=0)

# ---- Growth rates (indexed to first year) ----
summary_growth <- summary_df %>%
  group_by(metro_status) %>%
  arrange(year) %>%
  mutate(
    firms_index = total_firms / first(total_firms) * 100,
    emp_index   = total_emp / first(total_emp) * 100
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

p3 <- ggplot(summary_growth, aes(x = year, y = firms_index, color = metro_status)) +
  geom_line(size = 1.2) +
  labs(title = "Firm Growth Rate: Indexed to First Year", y = "Index (First Year = 100)", x = "Year") +
  theme_minimal() +
  expand_limits(y = 0)

p4 <- ggplot(summary_growth, aes(x = year, y = emp_index, color = metro_status)) +
  geom_line(size = 1.2) +
  labs(title = "Employment Growth Rate: Indexed to First Year", y = "Index (First Year = 100)", x = "Year") +
  theme_minimal() +
  expand_limits(y = 0)


print(p1)









Code
print(p2)









Code
print(p3)









Code
print(p4)









Code
# Compute growth rates pre vs post COVID
growth_summary <- summary_df %>%
  group_by(metro_status) %>%
  summarise(
    firms_growth = (last(total_firms) - first(total_firms)) / first(total_firms) * 100,
    emp_growth   = (last(total_emp) - first(total_emp)) / first(total_emp) * 100,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

print(growth_summary)


# A tibble: 2 × 3
  metro_status firms_growth emp_growth
  <chr>               <dbl>      <dbl>
1 metro               60.3        91.5
2 nonmetro             2.48       33.0


We can see that in metro areas the firms grew by 60% post pandemic and only 2.5% where as for the employment rate in non metro areas there has been a 92% growth and only a 32% growth in non metro areas. However according to the plots we cannot clearly comprehend the growth for the non metro regions so we index to first year(base year). By doing this we can see which area is growing faster proportionally.Non metro regions have a lesser growth rate compared to metro regions(We can see this more clearly after we indexed it with a base of 100).

title: Business Dynamics Database (BDS)
text:
Multi-establishment firms
Do rural areas have more single establishment firms vs multi-establishment firms?


Code
df <- pubdata::get("bds", "2022_met") %>%
  mutate(
    metro_status = case_when(
      metro == "M" ~ "metro",
      metro == "N" ~ "nonmetro",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(metro_status))

# Calculate single vs multi establishment counts
df_summary <- df %>%
  group_by(year, metro_status) %>%
  summarise(
    total_firms = sum(firms, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_estabs = sum(estabs, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    single_estab_firms = total_firms,                     # every firm has at least one estab
    multi_estab_extra  = total_estabs - total_firms,      # establishments beyond 1 per firm
    share_single_estab = total_firms / total_estabs * 100 # % of establishments that are single-unit
  )

print(df_summary)


# A tibble: 90 × 7
    year metro_status total_firms total_estabs single_estab_firms
   <int> <chr>              <int>        <int>              <int>
 1  1978 metro            3284606      4005231            3284606
 2  1978 nonmetro          283387       315444             283387
 3  1979 metro            3408383      4155226            3408383
 4  1979 nonmetro          291424       324013             291424
 5  1980 metro            3457634      4221684            3457634
 6  1980 nonmetro          290968       324005             290968
 7  1981 metro            3487558      4281982            3487558
 8  1981 nonmetro          289157       323219             289157
 9  1982 metro            3468666      4293546            3468666
10  1982 nonmetro          283596       318419             283596
# ℹ 80 more rows
# ℹ 2 more variables: multi_estab_extra <int>, share_single_estab <dbl>


Code
# Visualization: share of single-unit establishments
ggplot(df_summary, aes(x = year, y = share_single_estab, color = metro_status)) +
  geom_line(size = 1.2) +
  labs(
    title = "Share of Single-Establishment Firms",
    y = "Percent Single-Establishment",
    x = "Year"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









Rural areas continue to depend more on small, independent firms, while metros are more dominated by multi-location businesses. But both areas are trending toward fewer single-establishment firms over time, reflecting a national shift toward consolidation and chain expansion.

title: Tidy Public Data
text:
“Like families, tidy datasets are all alike but every messy dataset is messy in its own way” (Wickham, 2014).
pubdata is an R package that provides a minimalist interface to a wide range of publicly available datasets in a standardized tidy table form.

title: Tidy Public Data
text:
ls(): list available datasets
Without parameters, returns a list of all available data collections.

pubdata::ls()

[1] "bds"       "bea_fa"    "bea_io"    "bea_reg"   "chrr"      "ers_rural"
[7] "naics"     "nass"      "qcew"     


With detail = TRUE returns collections with descriptions.

pubdata::ls(detail = TRUE)









collection
description




bds
Business Dynamics Statistics (BDS)


bea_fa
BEA Fixed Assets Accounts


bea_io
Input-Output Tables, BEA


bea_reg
BEA Regional Economic Accounts


chrr
County Health Rankings and Roadmaps (CHRR)


ers_rural
Rurality classifications from the USDA Economic Research Service (ERS)


naics
North American Industry Classification System (NAICS)


nass
National Agricultural Statistics Service (NASS)


qcew
Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages (QCEW)






If collection is specified, returns the list of available dataset keys. For example, keys in the "ers_rural" collection:

pubdata::ls(collection = "ers_rural")

 [1] "raw_ruc_1974"     "raw_ruc_1983"     "raw_ruc_1993"     "raw_ruc_2003"    
 [5] "raw_ruc_2003pr"   "raw_ruc_2013"     "raw_ruc_2023"     "ruc_1974"        
 [9] "ruc_1983"         "ruc_1993"         "ruc_2003"         "ruc_2003pr"      
[13] "ruc_2013"         "ruc_2023"         "raw_ui_2003pr"    "raw_ui_2003_1993"
[17] "raw_ui_2013"      "raw_ui_2024"      "ui_2003pr"        "ui_2003_1993"    
[21] "ui_2013"          "ui_2024"          "raw_ruca_1990"    "raw_ruca_2000"   
[25] "raw_ruca_2010"    "raw_ruca_2010zip" "raw_ruca_2020"    "raw_ruca_2020zip"
[29] "ruca_1990"        "ruca_2000"        "ruca_2010"        "ruca_2010zip"    
[33] "ruca_2020"        "ruca_2020zip"     "raw_far_2000"     "raw_far_2010"    
[37] "far_2000"         "far_2010"        

# show descriptions and cache files
pubdata::ls(collection = "ers_rural", detail = TRUE) |>
  slice_sample(n = 6)











key
description
cache_file
cache_exists




raw_ruca_2020zip
2020 Rural-Urban Commuting Area Codes, ZIP code
raw/ruca_2020zip.xlsx
TRUE

1993 Rural-Urban Continuum Codes
raw/ruc_1993.xls
TRUE


ruca_2020zip
2020 Rural-Urban Commuting Area Codes, ZIP code
proc/ruca_2020zip.pq
TRUE


raw_ruca_2000
2000 Rural-Urban Commuting Area Codes, census tract
raw/ruca_2000.xls
TRUE

1990 Rural-Urban Commuting Area Codes, census tract
raw/ruca_1990.xls
TRUE


raw_ui_2024
2024 Urban Influence Codes
raw/ui_2024.xlsx
TRUE






Use pattern parameter to list keys that match a regex pattern within the data collection. For example, pattern "^ruc_" in the "ers_rural" collection will list all available Rural-Urban Continuum Code datasets from the USDA ERS rurality classifications collection.

pubdata::ls(collection = "ers_rural", pattern = "^ruc_")

[1] "ruc_1974"   "ruc_1983"   "ruc_1993"   "ruc_2003"   "ruc_2003pr"
[6] "ruc_2013"   "ruc_2023"

title: Tidy Public Data
section: meta(): show collection or dataset metadata
text:
meta(): show collection or dataset metadata
Obtain metadata of a collection or a specific dataset.
For collections, meta() will print:

desc: collection description
urls: a list of data source links
data_keys: the number of data keys within the collection


pubdata::meta(collection = "ers_rural", print = TRUE)

collection: ers_rural
desc: Rurality classifications from the USDA Economic Research Service (ERS)
urls:
  Home: https://www.ers.usda.gov/topics/rural-economy-population/rural-classifications/
  Rural-Urban Continuum Codes: https://www.ers.usda.gov/data-products/rural-urban-continuum-codes/
  Urban Influence Codes: https://www.ers.usda.gov/data-products/urban-influence-codes/
  Rural-Urban Commuting Area Codes: https://www.ers.usda.gov/data-products/rural-urban-commuting-area-codes/
  Frontier and Remote Area Codes: https://www.ers.usda.gov/data-products/frontier-and-remote-area-codes/
data_keys: 38


For datasets and raw data files, meta() will provide:

desc: dataset description
type: “raw” or “table”
path: local file path
depends: key of the raw data file
schema: dataset variables with their descriptions and data types
mask: key naming pattern, for internal use
keys: key parts parsed with mask, for internal use


pubdata::meta(collection = "ers_rural", key = "ruc_2023", print = TRUE)

desc: 2023 Rural-Urban Continuum Codes
type: table
path: proc/ruc_2023.pq
depends: raw_ruc_2023
schema:
  fips:
    desc: State+county FIPS code
    orig: FIPS
    type: character
  state:
    desc: State or territory abbreviation
    orig: State
    type: character
  county_name:
    desc: County or county-equivalent name
    orig: County_Name
    type: character
  population_2020:
    desc: Total population 2020
    orig: Population_2020
    type: integer
  ruc_code_2023:
    desc: 2023 Rural-Urban Continuum Code
    orig: RUCC_2023
    type: character
  ruc_desc_2023:
    desc: Description for 2023 codes
    orig: Description
    type: character


meta() invisibly returns the information as a nested list, which can be used to programmatically extract specific components. Set print = FALSE to silence the output.

ruc_2023_meta <- pubdata::meta(collection = "ers_rural", key = "ruc_2023", print = FALSE)
ruc_2023_meta$schema$ruc_desc_2023

$desc
[1] "Description for 2023 codes"

$orig
[1] "Description"

$type
[1] "character"

title: Tidy Public Data
text:
get(): retrieve a dataset
get() for raw data keys downloads the file to local cache and returns its path.

pubdata::get(collection = "ers_rural", key = "raw_ruc_2023")

[1] "/Users/anton/work/pubdata_cache/ers_rural/raw/ruc_2023.xlsx"


For tables, on the first call get() downloads the source dependency file if necessary, reads it, cleans the data and stores it to a local Parquet file. Subsequent calls load the Parquet file directly.

pubdata::get(collection = "ers_rural", key = "ruc_2023") |>
  head(10)

title: Tidy Public Data
text:
path(): dataset file path
A common usage pattern with large datasets is to query a subset from disk without loading the entire data frame into memory. Once the dataset has been built with get(), the path() function provides a shorthand to the Parquet file location on disk.

# slow, run once
# pubdata::get("qcew", "naics_ann_2024")

# fast
pubdata::path("qcew", "naics_ann_2024") |>
  arrow::open_dataset() |>
  filter(area_fips == "55025", agglvl_code == "74", own_code == "5") |>
  select(industry_code, annual_avg_estabs, annual_avg_emplvl) |>
  collect() |>
  left_join(pubdata::get("qcew", "meta_naics_ind"), by = "industry_code") |>
  select(sector = industry_title, establishments = annual_avg_estabs, employment = annual_avg_emplvl)

title: BEA Regional Economic Accounts
text:
On this page
   
  
  Overview
  Tables
  Columns
  Examples
  


Overview

The regional economic accounts tell us about the geographic distribution of U.S. economic activity and growth. The estimates of gross domestic product by state and state and local area personal income, and the accompanying detail, provide a consistent framework for analyzing and comparing individual state and local area economies.

Data source links.

title: Rurality classifications from the USDA Economic Research Service (ERS)
text:
On this page
   
  
  Overview
  Tables
  Columns
  Examples
  


Overview

USDA, Economic Research Service (ERS) researchers and others who analyze conditions in “rural” America most often study conditions in nonmetropolitan (nonmetro) areas, defined on the basis of counties. Counties are a standard geographic unit for collecting economic data and for conducting research to track and explain regional population and economic trends.


In addition to conducting research that uses the basic metro-nonmetro dichotomy, ERS has developed multi-level county classifications to measure rurality in more detail and to assess the economic and social diversity of nonmetro America. Some of these classification schemes have been used to determine eligibility for Federal programs that assist rural areas.


For some research and program applications, counties are too large to accurately distinguish rural and urban settlement patterns. The U.S. Department of Commerce, Bureau of the Census uses much smaller geographic building blocks to define rural areas as open country and settlements with fewer than 5,000 residents and fewer than 2,000 housing units.

Data source links.

Home
Rural-Urban Continuum Codes
Urban Influence Codes
Rural-Urban Commuting Area Codes

title: National Agricultural Statistics Service (NASS)
text:
On this page
   
  
  Overview
  Tables
  Variables
  Examples
  


Overview

The USDA’s National Agricultural Statistics Service (NASS) conducts hundreds of surveys every year and prepares reports covering virtually every aspect of U.S. agriculture. Production and supplies of food and fiber, prices paid and received by farmers, farm labor and wages, farm finances, chemical use, and changes in the demographics of U.S. producers are only a few examples.
